title: Do Corporations Retain Too Much Cash? Evidence from a Natural Experiment
abstract: Corporations have accumulated record amounts of cash. We study whether firms’ cash retention has been excessive by examining a Korean reform that introduced a new tax on earnings retained as cash. Difference-in-differences tests show that treated firms reduce cash retention and instead increase payouts and investments. Market participants react favorably to the reform, consistent with excessive cash retention. The valuation effects and the alternative uses of the cash are associated with behavioral and agency frictions. Firms that are more subject to behavioral biases increase payouts and experience higher valuations, while poorly governed firms allocate more to investment and experience relatively lower valuations.

author.Woojin Kim: Seoul National University, ECGI, South Korea

Corporations’ cash balances have risen dramatically all over the world (Pinkowitz, Stulz, and Williamson 2015). This phenomenon is often referred to as a “corporate savings glut” or “cash hoarding,” implying that firms’ cash accumulation has been excessive. However, companies have many reasons for accumulating cash, some that indeed can be value-destroying (e.g., cash buildup resulting from misaligned incentives between managers and owners), but others that are value-enhancing. For example, some reasons why it recently may have been optimal for firms to accumulate more cash include greater precautionary savings motives (Opler et al. 1999;Bates, Kahle, and Stulz 2009), lower opportunity costs (Azar, Kagy, and Schmalz 2016), increased competition (Lyandres and Palazzo 2016), costs of repatriating foreign earnings (Foley et al. 2007;Faulkender, Hankins, and Petersen 2019), or higher levels of intangible capital (Falato et al. Forthcoming). Thus, it remains an open question whether firms have been retaining “too much” cash to the detriment of value or if firms’ cash accumulation, while high, has been at the correct level.

To evaluate whether firms retain too much cash, we ideally need an exogenous shock to their cash retention policies and a way to measure the effect on valuations. If a firm retains the right amount of cash, it weighs the marginal cost of retaining an additional dollar against the marginal benefit (e.g.,Opler et al. 1999;Almeida, Campello, and Weisbach 2004).1The marginal value of retaining a dollar as cash is then equal to the value of this dollar’s alternative use—for example, paid out to investors or invested in projects. In that case, any change in cash retention from this level will reduce firm value. However, if a firm retains too much cash such that the alternative use is more valuable than keeping the marginal dollar as cash, then a decrease in retention can raise the firm’s value.

The main goal of this paper is to shed light on this question using a natural experiment. Specifically, we study the effects of a 2014 tax reform in South Korea that the government introduced with the aim of reducing firms’ cash retention. The reform involved a 10%surtax on “excess retention” out of firms’ earnings that is not spent on investments, wage increases, or shareholder payouts.2The tax applied only to firms with book equity above 50 billion Korean won (roughly US$50 million), as well as to firms that were part of large business groups called “chaebols.” We refer to these firms that were subject to the new tax as the “treated” firms.3Non-chaebol firms below the 50 billion won threshold were exempted from the new tax. Crucially, this threshold is unrelated to any other government reform we are aware of that could differentially affect firms around the same time, and the tax rate is sufficiently large to have significant “bite.”

We conduct three separate analyses to map out the effects of this natural experiment. First, we study the effects on firms’ cash policies using a difference-in-differences framework by comparing changes in cash retention from two years before the reform (2013–14) to two years after (2015–16) between the treated and untreated firms. Second, we examine the valuation impact of the reform based on an event study framework by measuring how the treated firms’ valuations were differentially affected around key dates when the reform was proposed and passed. Third, because earnings not retained as cash have to be spent on something else, we use the difference-in-differences methodology to study the effects on alternative uses of cash flows, including spending on investments, wages, and payouts.

We begin by showing that the treated firms responded to the reform by significantly cutting their cash accumulation rate by around half. This corresponds to an average reduction of about 4 billion won (US$4 million) per treated firm annually, or 16 trillion won (US$16 billion) in aggregate per year. Effecting such a change was the stated goal of the reform, and this result is important to establish as a first step: without any change in cash retention, it would be less relevant to study changes to valuations and alternative uses.

We next study the effects on valuations. Conceptually, if a firm’s cash policies were optimal, then the new tax will be distortive and result in lower valuations, either from distorted decisions if the firm retains less cash to avoid the tax, from higher taxes paid if it does not make sufficient changes to avoid the tax, or both. However, if the marginal value of the alternative use of a dollar was higher than the value of retaining it, the reform could have a positive value effect by pushing firms to save less. Our findings show that the treated firms’ cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) around the key dates were positive, around 2%⁠. This raised the aggregate market value of the treated firms that were publicly listed by around 20 trillion won (around US$20 billion). Consistent with better allocation of marginal dollars between cash retention and alternative uses, we also find that the treated firms’ “value of cash,” based on the methodology ofFaulkender and Wang (2006), moved from significantly below one dollar (62 cents) toward closer to one (92 cents).

Toward what alternative uses was this money allocated? We find that the treated firms, on average, increased payouts by around 30%⁠, corresponding to an increase of around US$6 billion annually to investors. Further, capital expenditures increased by 20%–30%⁠, and the treated firms also raised wages. Part of this increase in spending by the treated firms was also financed with more debt issuance. Across all of the dimensions we study—cash retention, valuations, and alternative uses—we find stronger effects for firms affected more intensely by the reform due to their having higher earnings.

We finally examine the role of two possible mechanisms for why many firms might have engaged in excessive cash retention before the reform—behavioral biases and agency conflicts. First, we posit that excessive cash retention could be related to behavioral biases resulting from experiences from previous financial crises when large cash buffers were important, especially if managers overweight the significance of such experiences.4Second, poor corporate governance and agency conflicts can also cause excessive cash retention if managers have a personal incentive to hold on to excess liquidity (Bertrand and Mullainathan 2003;Gao, Harford, and Li 2013;Nikolov and Whited 2014;Isakov, Pérignon, and Weisskopf 2021). Consistent with a possible role for both mechanisms, we find that firms with poor governance and external-capital-dependent firms with past experiences from the Asian financial crisis tended to retain more cash in the years leading up to the reform.

We then examine how these two mechanisms relate to firms’ and investors’ responses to the reform. Regarding the role of behavioral biases, our findings show that firms with past experiences from the Asian financial crisis responded more strongly by reducing their cash retention more and increasing payouts more than other firms. They also experienced more positive valuation effects, implying that the tax “nudge” to retain less cash benefitted these firms more. When we compare treated firms with better versus worse corporate governance, we first find that both groups of firms tended to save less after the reform. However, when it comes to alternative uses and valuations, poorly governed firms tended only to increase investments and did not experience positive valuation effects, consistent with these higher investments not being positive-NPV. By contrast, well-governed treated firms tended to spend more on payouts, and these firms did experience positive valuation effects. One important policy-related takeaway from our findings is that pushing firms with excessive retention to save less cash is not a silver bullet because the valuation effects depend on what they choose to do with the money instead, and ultimately on the underlying motivations behind these firms’ cash policies.

While this Korean reform is unique in recent times, other countries have also weighed similar policies, including Japan and China.5Even though the institutional context in each country differs, the two mechanisms for excessive retention we examine—agency problems and behavioral biases—are likely relevant in many other countries. For example, Korean firms tend to be controlled by powerful families and business groups, with limited protection for outside investors, but firms in many other parts of the world are similar in these dimensions with the notable exception of the United States and the United Kingdom (Morck, Wolfenzon, and Yeung 2005). And while the Asian financial crisis hit Korean firms hard, other Asian, Latin American, and European countries have experienced similar financial crises in recent decades, in addition to the global impacts of the 2008 financial crisis and the 2020 COVID-19 episode. South Korean firms’ cash and investment levels have also been similar to the median among developed and middle-income countries, which further informs the external validity of our findings.

We take multiple complementary approaches to support the parallel trends assumption in the difference-in-differences framework. First, an important trade-off relates to narrowing the sample around the 50 billion won threshold, which helps strengthen internal validity, versus allowing a broader sample of firms around the threshold, which helps support generality and sufficient statistical power. To balance these perspectives, we repeat our analysis across three alternative samples: all firms, firms in a narrower range around the threshold, and a one-to-one matched sample, and we find consistent results across all three approaches. Second, we find no significant differential pre-trends between the treated and untreated firms. Third, we employ a regression discontinuity difference-in-differences (RD-DD) framework by controlling for the distance of firms’ book equity value to the treatment threshold, and also control for other time-varying covariates such as assets and leverage. Finally, we exploit a placebo test based on the feature that the 50 billion won book equity threshold only affects whether non-chaebol firms are treated, but that this threshold does not determine treatment among chaebol firms.

This setting represents an opportunity to study the effects of a government policy squarely aimed at curtailing corporate cash retention. A related setting studied in the literature is the American Jobs Creation Act (AJCA) of 2004, which allowed firms to repatriate foreign earnings without paying the full U.S. corporate tax rate. The underlying motivation behind the AJCA was similar to that of the Korean reform in that it sought to encourage firms to invest more instead of retaining cash.Blouin and Krull (2009),Dharmapala, Foley, and Forbes (2011), andFaulkender and Petersen (2012)examine what firms do when they enjoy cheaper access to their foreign cash after the AJCA.Blouin and Krull (2009)andDharmapala, Foley, and Forbes (2011)argue that the AJCA primarily resulted in higher payouts, whileFaulkender and Petersen (2012)contend that there was an increase in domestic investment, especially for constrained firms. An important benefit of the current study is that it allows examining valuation consequences to speak to the optimality of firms’ policies: Since the AJCA was a tax cut that removed a tax wedge between foreign and domestic cash, it cannot readily speak to whether firms’ foreign cash was excessive because both the tax cut and any improvement in cash policies have the same (positive) effect on value. By contrast, because the current study involves a tax increase, we can distinguish more effectively whether cash holdings are excessive since the value effect of the tax increase and any positive effect from firms’ responses act in opposite directions. Another difference is that the AJCA was a one-time windfall opportunity to repatriate cash, while the current setting represents a more permanent change in tax policy. This is important as firms are likely to make a different trade-off between investments and payouts depending on the duration of the reform (e.g.,Blanchard, Lopez-de-Silanes, and Shleifer 1994).

A second similar historical reform was the undistributed profits tax (UPT), which raised taxes on retained earnings in the United States between 1936 and 1937.Poterba, Hall, and Hubbard (1987)show that the UPT resulted in higher payouts.Christie and Nanda (1994)further find that the UPT was associated with positive abnormal returns, arguing that the UPT alleviated agency problems, whileCalomiris and Hubbard (1995)instead argue that firms’ responses are more consistent with information frictions. The empirical setting in the current study offers two important advantages relative to the UPT. First, the Korean experiment has a control group of firms not subject to the new tax, while the UPT had no such natural control group.6Second, the current experiment takes place in recent times when the nature of firms and their relation with investors differ substantially from the 1930s.

Finally, our study contributes to the literature on the value of corporate cash.Faulkender and Wang (2006)estimate that the marginal value of cash is $0.94 on average, but this varies with firms’ financial constraints, andDittmar and Mahrt-Smith (2007)find that the value of cash is lower for poorly governed firms.Harford (1999)shows that cash-rich firms tend to make more value-destroying acquisitions. Our results contribute to the view that corporate cash policies can be suboptimal, and we further build on this literature by examining this question through the lens of a natural experiment.
On July 16, 2014, the newly appointed Korean minister of strategy and finance, Choi Kyoung-Hwan, announced a plan for a tax reform that included a new tax on corporate cash retention. The stated aim of the new tax was to incentivize corporations to invest more and to boost domestic consumer demand. An underlying idea was that money on corporate balance sheets instead could be put to more productive use in the form of new investments or more consumer spending.7

The reform has several features that make it well suited for empirically analyzing its impact: It was enacted quickly, it applied only to a subset of firms, and the tax rate was significant. The official proposal for the reform was made on August 6, 2014, the National Assembly passed the law on December 2, 2014, and it took effect on January 1, 2015. The timeline from the initial proposal of the law to when it came into effect thus gave firms little time to react to the reform in advance.

The reform did not target all firms but applied only to those that satisfied at least one of two criteria: (i) firms with book equity greater than 50 billion won as of the previous fiscal year, or (ii) firms belonging to a chaebol. We will refer to these as the treated firms. Firms that did not satisfy either criterion were not subject to the new tax. For the treated firms, the reform imposed a 10%tax on “excess retention,” which in turn was defined as earnings above a specific threshold after allowing for deductions for payouts, investments, and wage increases.8A 10-percentage-point tax on excess retention out of earnings is significant relative to the standard corporate tax rate, which was 22%during our sample period.

There were two possible formulas for calculating the tax, which we refer to as Rule A and Rule B. Each treated firm could choose which rule it would follow but would be bound to its chosen rule for at least the next three years. These rules were the following:

(Rule A):tax amount=     [net profit×0.8 – (investments+wage incr.+payouts) ]×0.1(Rule B):tax amount=     [net profit×0.3 – (wage incr.+payouts)]×0.1

Based on Rule A, treated firms that spent more than 80%of their earnings on payouts, investments, or wage increases during a year would avoid the new tax, while those that retained more than 20%after these deductions would be taxed at 10%on the residual. Rule B did not allow for any deduction for investment but required firms to spend at least 30%of earnings on payouts and wage increases to avoid the tax. Rule A was thus relatively more favorable for firms with capital expenditures above 50%of earnings, while Rule B was more favorable for lower-investment firms.9We do not have data on which rule each treated firm chose, but we can calculate a lower bound on the tax amounts based on the most favorable rule for each firm.

While some firms already paid out or invested enough of their earnings to avoid the tax, a substantial fraction of firms did not, meaning that the new tax had “bite” for most firms. Specifically, 58%of the treated firms in our sample would have paid this tax during the two years before the reform was enacted (2013 or 2014) if it had been in place then, based on applying the most favorable rule for each firm. We describe the treated firms and the rule choices in more detail in Section2.4.

This reform was controversial. Despite widespread political support, the reform received significant pushback from the private sector. On the one hand, policymakers generally agreed that Korean firms were excessively accumulating cash and that directing more of this money elsewhere would benefit the broader economy. On the other hand, some observers raised doubts about the efficacy of the reform. These views were based primarily on the assumption that firms were already choosing their financial and investment policies optimally so that any additional taxation or “forced” spending on payouts or investments would only drive firms to make suboptimal decisions, thereby destroying value. It was also argued that the reform would effectively increase the total corporate tax rate, eventually suppressing corporate investment.
article.2. Data and Empirical Strategy:
This section first describes the data construction and the empirical frameworks for measuring the effects on firms’ responses and valuations. We next report statistics on the number of treated and untreated firms. Finally, we report summary statistics and compare Korean firms with those in several other developed and middle-income countries.

2.1 Data and sample construction

Most of the data for this study, including accounting information, stock prices, and other firm characteristics, are collected from DataGuide, a comprehensive database on both public and private firms in Korea. We use data from the Korean Fair Trade Commission’s (KFTC) annual designation of large business groups to determine which firms belong to a chaebol.10We obtain firm-level corporate governance measures from the Korea Corporate Governance Service.

We apply several filters to refine the sample. First, we eliminate firm-year observations for which data on book equity are missing, as this variable determines treatment. We exclude financial firms and utilities since these firms’ cash and investment policies are regulated. We also eliminate the smallest firms (those with total assets of less than 1 billion won, or approximately US$1 million), and a few firms with very negative capital expenditures (less than –10%of total assets). Finally, we drop three specific firms—Hyundai Motors, Kia Motors, and Hyundai Mobis; these firms made substantial investments in real estate during our sample period, but these investments had been planned long before the tax reform and are therefore not plausibly related to the reform itself.11Our final full sample includes 20,916 unique firms.

2.2 Measuring the impact on financial and investment policies

To analyze the effects of the reform on firms’ financial and investment policies, we employ a difference-in-differences methodology. This analysis compares firms treated by the reform to firms that were not treated, from two years before the reform (2013–14) to two years after (2015–16). We estimate the following baseline model:

y i , t = θ + β 0 Treated i + β 1 After t + β 2 Treated i × After t + η ′ ⋅ X i , t + φ i + τ t + ψ j , t + ε i , t , (1)

whereiindexes firms,jindexes industries, andtindexes years.12

yi,tdenotes the outcome variables, including cash accumulation (measured as net changes to cash balances), payouts, investments, and wage increases. We principally study these outcomes because they were explicitly targeted by the reform. The indicator variableTreateddenotes whether a firm was subject to the new tax; as discussed in Section1, theTreatedfirms are either (i) firms with book equity greater than 50 billion won as of the previous fiscal year, or (ii) firms that are part of a chaebol, as designated by the Korea Fair Trade Commission (KFTC).Afteris an indicator variable that takes the value of one for the post-reform years (2015 and 2016). The main coefficient of interest isβ2⁠, which captures the average treatment effect of the reform on the outcome variables.Xis a vector of firm-level control variables.13φidenotes firm fixed effects, which account for any time-invariant differences across firms;τtdenotes year fixed effects to control for any time-varying economy-wide shocks; andψj,tdenotes industry-by-year fixed effects to control for any industry-specific shocks or other time-varying omitted variables that affect a particular industry.14We cluster standard errors throughout our analysis at the firm level for standalone firms or at the chaebol level for firms that belong to a chaebol.

The critical identification assumption in a difference-in-differences setting is the parallel trends assumption—that the treated and untreated firms would have followed similar trends in the outcomes in the absence of treatment. An advantage of the empirical setting is that the 50 billion won threshold is plausibly unrelated to other policies or omitted variables that could drive differential changes between the treated and untreated firms’ financial and investment policies around the time of the reform.

We might nevertheless be concerned that a firm’s book equity could be spuriously related to differential changes in the outcome variables and thereby invalidate the parallel trends assumption; that is, if firms of different sizes were to change their financial and investment policies in systematically different ways in thePostperiod but for some reason unrelated to the reform. We employ several complementary approaches to address this concern. Most notably, we analyze three alternative samples of firms, where each alternative strikes a different balance between internal validity versus generality: (i) the full sample, (ii) a sample limited to relatively smaller firms with book equity between 10 billion and 90 billion won in a symmetric window around the threshold, and (iii) a sample with one-to-one matching between each treated firm and its nearest neighbor. In the matching procedure, we require an exact match on industry and publicly listed status, and within that set, we select the nearest neighbor based on the Mahalanobis distance of pre-reformsales,sales growth,net income, andleverage.Table A1in theInternet Appendixcompares firm characteristics between the matched treated and untreated firms as of 2013. The firms are quite similar, except that the treated firms are slightly larger (higher sales and higher assets). These differences are expected since treatment is based on book equity, and book equity is correlated with other size measures like sales and assets. While the difference-in-differences analysis does not demand that such characteristics across groups be identical (any differences in levels are also accounted for with firm fixed effects), this comparison nevertheless shows that these treated and untreated firms are quite similar across many observable characteristics. As we will describe in more detail in Section7, we also employ a battery of additional robustness tests by exploiting differences in treatment intensity, separately analyzing chaebol and non-chaebol firms, examining pre-trends, accounting for additional control variables, and implementing placebo tests.

2.3 Measuring valuation effects

To determine whether the reform raised or lowered valuations for the treated firms, we employ an event study methodology that captures market participants’ reactions. These event returns plausibly capture investors’ expectations regarding how firms would respond to the law and the consequences for firm value.

We start by calculating cumulative abnormal returns (⁠CARi⁠), in separate three-day windows from one day before to one day after each of two key event dates: the proposal of the law on August 6, 2014, and the law’s passage on December 2, 2014, and we then sum the abnormal returns across these two events (six event days in total) for each firm. The reason we focus on the proposal and passage dates is that—even though rumors of a law in this spirit existed before the formal proposal—these dates are when information indicating which firms would be treated and which firms would not be treated was first laid out and then affirmed through legislation. To examine whether the treated firms’ values differentially changed around these dates, we then regressCARion whether firmiisTreated:

CAR i = β 0 + β 1 Treated i + ψ j + ε i , (2)

These regressions include industry fixed effects,ψj⁠, to account for any industry-wide return differences on these days; this implies that the regression effectively compares differences in returns between treated and untreated firms within the same industry on the days when investors learned which of these firms would be treated.

This methodology crucially helps account for investor expectations regarding the features of the reform, for example, what the tax rate would be or what deductions would be allowed. To illustrate this feature, suppose investors expected a reform that would destroy 5%of value and that such a reform had a 50%likelihood of actually being passed, so that a negative 2.5%value consequence is “priced in” for all firms. Suppose investors then learned more details about the reform—that it would destroy only 3%of value for treated firms and that it would pass with certainty. Then, if we only consider the returns for the treated firms, we would see those firms experience negative returns of –0.5%(going from a probabilistic priced-in effect of –2.5%to –3%⁠), which would be an underestimate of the actual value consequence of –3%⁠.

However, if we compare the differences in CARs between treated and untreated firms on the dates when investors find out which firms will be treated, we can learn about the actual valuation effect regardless of preexisting expectations that otherwise can cloud the interpretation. Continuing on the previous example, while the treated firms would experience a negative return of –0.5%⁠, the firms that investors learn would not be treated would experience a positive return of 2.5%(going from a priced-in expectation of –2.5%to zero). The difference in returns between the treated and untreated firms is then –3%⁠, which exactly uncovers the actual value effect for the treated firms. For this method to measure the effect precisely, we need all firms to be subject to the same ex ante probability of being treated; a potential caveat then is that the very largest firms in the economy (e.g., Samsung Electronics), whose cash policies much of the political discussion revolved around, likely would be treated under any reform. In addition to examining results in the full sample, we therefore also examine abnormal returns where we limit firms to those with book equity between 10 billion and 90 billion won; when we focus on this narrower range, we have no evidence to suggest that there existed any differential expectations whether firms on either side of the threshold would be more likely to be treated or not.

As an alternative to this event study framework, we also use a long-short calendar-time portfolio approach to measure abnormal returns, which we describe in more detail in AppendixA.

These statistics show that the firms on average (at the median) hold 10.66%(5.72%⁠) of assets in cash, and the level of cash is growing at a rate of 1.08%of assets. Payouts are 0.75%of assets, mostly in the form of dividends, and investments are 3.05%of assets on average. We present summary statistics separately for public and private firms inTable A2in theInternet Appendix. Overall, public firms in our sample tend to hold more cash than private firms, which is similar to findings in other countries.17

One caveat of any empirical setting is that the findings may not generalize to other contexts or countries. To help evaluate the external validity of the current setting, we next compare Korean firms’ cash and investment policies with those in other developed and middle-income countries. For this analysis, we use data across firms in 30 countries from the Bureau van Dijk (BvD) Orbis database between 2013 and 2016. To better compare countries with different reporting thresholds, we filter out small firms with less than US$10 million in total assets. We include public and private firms whenever available. In a few countries such as the United States, these data only include public firms, which skews their statistics relative to the other countries. These statistics are reported inTable A3in theInternet Appendix.

Table A3shows that Korean firms in this sample on average (at the median) hold 9.2%(4.2%⁠) of assets in cash.18These numbers are comparable to the global average (median) in this group of countries, which is 11.4%(4.4%⁠). Further, the average level of investment among Korean firms of 2.32%is also quite close to the cross-country average of 2.46%⁠. Some of the countries that appear most similar to South Korea along these dimensions include France, Indonesia, New Zealand, Norway, Sweden, and the United Kingdom.

In Section6.2, we study the role of agency problems as a possible mechanism behind excessive cash retention. To assess the external validity of this channel, it is important to consider how corporate governance in South Korea compares with other developed and middle-income countries. To this end,Table A3in theInternet Appendixshows country-level statistics on two widely used investor protection measures. First, based on the Anti-Director-Rights Index ofLa Porta et al. (1998), Korea scores 2, one notch below the average in this group of countries. However, Korea is far from an outlier on this measure as it scores the same as several other countries, including Austria, Denmark, Indonesia, and the Netherlands, and better than many countries, such as Belgium, Germany, and Italy. Second, based on the Anti-Self-Dealing Index ofDjankov et al. (2008), the score of South Korea (0.47) is close to the cross-sectional average in this group of countries (0.53). In sum, while evaluating the external validity of any empirical setting is always challenging, these statistics show that the Korean firms in our sample share similar characteristics as firms in many other countries across important dimensions such as their cash and investments policies and quality of corporate governance.

Table 3shows that the coefficients onTreated×Afterare significantly negative, implying that the treated firms responded to the reform by significantly reducing their cash retention compared with untreated firms. This result remains consistent across different sample definitions and types of fixed effects, and holds regardless of whether we include long-term marketable securities as part of cash. Overall, these results show that the reform had a significant “bite” as a shock to the treated firms’ cash policies in the intended direction, which motivates analyzing the value consequences and alternative uses in the next sections.
article.4. Valuation consequences:
4.1 Event study analysis

What effects did the reform have on firm value? Conceptually, we can think of two different effects. First, there will be a direct negative effect because of higher taxes for those treated firms that do not make sufficient adjustments to move below the taxable threshold (as defined by Rule A or Rule B, described in Section1). Second, there will also be an indirect effect on value because of how firms respond by changing their financial and investment policies (e.g., by retaining less cash). If firms’ cash policies were optimal before the reform, then the valuation effects will be unambiguously negative, both because of the direct cost of higher taxes and because the reform distorts cash retention from the previous level. However, if firms were retaining cash excessively before the reform and investors anticipate that the reform would cause the treated firms to make value-improving changes to their financial and investment policies, we might expect a counteracting positive “indirect” effect on value; in that case, the net effect on value can be either negative or positive depending on which effect dominates.19

To analyze the effect on value, we begin by employing the event study methodology. We report the results, based on estimating Equation (2), inTable 4.

Table 4shows that the treated firms’ cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) around the days when the new law was proposed and passed were positive. This result implies that market participants believed that firms were saving cash excessively before the reform and that incentivizing firms to retain less cash would raise their values on net.20

The economic magnitude of this effect is around 1.7%–2%higher firm value. There are around 1,200 treated public firms in the sample with an average market capitalization of around US$1 billion, implying a value increase of almost US$20 million per firm or around US$25 billion in aggregate. The estimated effect on value is similar regardless of whether we include the full sample of public firms (columns 1–2), focus on the narrower sample around the threshold (columns 3–4), or limit the sample to matched treated-untreated pairs (columns 5–6). These effects also remain similar when including industry fixed effects.21In AppendixA, instead of an event study methodology, we use a long-short calendar-time-portfolio-based approach to measure the abnormal returns around the event dates, followingEckbo, Nygaard, and Thorburn (2022). The results are similar, with valuations increasing around 1.4–2%for the treated firms.

To reconcile the magnitude of the abnormal returns of almost 2%with the reduced cash retention rate of around 0.3–0.5 percentage points (normalized by assets), it is important to recognize that the effects on cash retention represent a change in annual flows; the average treated public firm has assets of US$2 billion, so for these firms, the drop in cash retention is around US$10 million per year. In contrast, the 2%change in market value, or US$20 million per firm, captures the present value from the cumulative changes in firm policies (net of the direct cost of taxes) going forward.22

The fact that value effects are net positive also supports the arguments made by many investors when the reform was passed that the reform would raise valuations. For example, according to a 2014 article by Korea JoongAng Daily, one of the country’s three major newspapers, “the majority of institutional investors expect that the reform would positively impact the stock market. Equity investors predict that dividends, in particular, will increase in response to the reform, which could help support higher valuations.”23These results also echo findings from the undistributed profits tax of 1936–37 in the United States, as reported byChristie and Nanda (1994), who find a positive market reaction of about 1%associated with that reform.

4.2 Effects on the marginal value of cash

We next examine whether the reform significantly affected the marginal value of cash. Prior evidence indicates that investors view a marginal dollar in a firm’s hands as worth less than a dollar (Faulkender and Wang 2006;Dittmar and Mahrt-Smith 2007). If the reform caused the treated firms to adopt better cash policies, we would expect the marginal value of a dollar of cash for the treated firms to increase from below $1 to closer to $1.

To examine this hypothesis, we employ the methodology byFaulkender and Wang (2006)and estimate the value of cash by regressing annual excess stock returns on annual changes in cash (⁠ΔCashi,t⁠) and other control variables (⁠Xi,t⁠). To measure how the marginal value of cash changed for the treated firms around the reform, we interact the changes in cash with indicators forTreated,After, andTreated×After:

Excess Return i , t = θ + β 1 Δ Cash i , t + β 2 Treated i + β 3 After t + β 4 Treated i × After t + β 5 Δ Cash i , t × Treated i + β 6 Δ Cash i , t × After t + β 7 Δ Cash i , t × Treated i × After t + η ′ ⋅ X i , t + ε i , t (3)

The control variablesXi,tare the same as inFaulkender and Wang (2006), and these control variables are also interacted withTreated,After, andTreated×After. Because this methodology generally requires more data than the previous diff-in-diff tests, we extend the sample period back to 2011. We exclude 2014 when returns are potentially endogenous to the value of cash, which means that the pre-period is 2011–2013 and the post-period is 2015–2016.24The main coefficient of interest isβ7⁠, which tests whether the value of cash changed differentially for the treated firms after the reform. The results are reported inTable 5.

article.5. Alternative Uses: Payouts, Investment:
We next examine the effects on payouts, investments, and wage increases. These represent alternative uses of the firm’s marginal cash. Of course, these categories (payouts, investments, and wages) do not exhaust all the possible uses of cash—just as saving less does not represent the only source of cash flow—which in turn implies that the sum across these alternative uses will not precisely equal the change in cash retention. These specific outcome categories are nevertheless of particular interest as the reform explicitly targeted them through allowed deductions (as discussed in Section1). At the end of this section, we also analyze the effect on net debt issuance, which is an important potential source of cash flows in addition to retaining less cash out of earnings.

In columns 3–4 and 5–6, we restrict the sample to a narrower bandwidth around the threshold and the matched treated-control pairs, respectively, and find similar results. We next study the effects on investments and wage increases.26In panel B ofTable 6, we report results for the effects on investments. We measure investments as changes in tangible assets plus depreciation, scaled by lagged total assets. The results show that the treated firms substantially increased their investments relative to the untreated firms after the reform. The economic magnitudes are also large—around 0.5%–0.7%of assets—representing a 20%increase compared to the average level of investment in the sample (3%of assets).27Panel B ofFigure 2shows this finding graphically.

Only the treated firms that elect “Rule A” (defined in Section1) get a deduction for investments. We thus expect the effects on investment to be concentrated among the firms that chose this rule. While we do not observe which rule each firm chose, we would nevertheless expect that firms sought to pick the rule that minimized their tax bill and that, even if firms do not have perfect foresight and always pick correctly, more firms got this choice right than wrong. Thus, to proxy for the firm’s choice, we define an indicator variablePrefer Rule Aas one if a firm was better off ex post (in 2015) with Rule A. Then we focus on treated firms and run difference-in-differences regressions depending on whether the firm was better off with Rule A. The results inTable A7in theInternet Appendixshow that the treated firms that were better off ex post with Rule A increased their investments significantly more than treated firms that were better off with Rule B or indifferent.

Next, we study the effects of the reform on wage increases. We measure wage increases as changes in firms’ non-executive wage bills, scaled by lagged total assets. The results in panel C ofTable 6indicate that the treated firms raised wages more after the reform. Panel C ofFigure 2also shows this finding graphically.28

Observing any investment or wage response at all may seem puzzling, given that firms could easily avoid the new tax by paying out money to shareholders instead. The fact that firms increased investments implies that managers viewed these investments as sufficiently valuable to dominate the alternative of paying out more money to shareholders. Firms should not do new investment projects unless they are positive-NPV, but if they are positive-NPV, the question arises why the firms did not do these projects earlier. One possibility is that these results reflect a preexisting availability of positive-NPV projects that previously had gone unfunded, possibly due to financial frictions that result in a high value of internal liquidity (Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988). However, in that case, we should observe negative consequences for value because the shadow value of retention was higher than the value of investment as the alternative. A second possibility is that some of these investments may be inefficient and reflect possible agency problems—a question we will return to in Section6.2.

Finally, inTable A9in theInternet Appendix, we show that the treated firms issued more debt (net of repayment of existing debt). This implies that the treated firms’ “net cash” retention (change in cash minus debt) declined by even more than that of the gross cash (which we studied in Section3). The increase in net debt issuances by treated firms also helps explain why the total combined increases in payouts and investments by treated firms (Table 6) are higher than the decreases in cash retention (Table 3). The increase in debt issuance partially reflects an incentive to fund more investment externally, allowing firms to pay out or invest even more than what firms could have done with internal funding alone.
article.6. Channels:
If retaining less cash raises firms’ values, why were firms retaining so much cash in the first place? In this section, we examine two mechanisms—behavioral biases and agency conflicts—that can help explain why some firms might have excessively retained cash in the first place. We particularly focus on how these channels relate to firms’ and investors’ responses to the reform. Our goal in this analysis is not to run a “horse race” between mutually exclusive or competing hypotheses but to identify possible channels that could independently cause excess retention among some firms.

6.1 Behavioral biases

The first channel we examine for why some of the treated firms may have exhibited excessive cash retention before the reform relates to behavioral biases caused by past experiences. We focus on the role of memories from the 1997 Asian financial crisis, which represented a severe negative shock to many firms in Korea. Experiencing this crisis may result in preferences to accumulate excessive cash buffers, especially if managers overestimate the likelihood of the next crisis, for example, because of availability bias (Tversky and Kahneman 1973). A memory from the crisis is likely to be especially salient for firms that depend heavily on external financing.

These kinds of biases have been supported in the literature. For example, previous studies have documented a link between experiences of severe recessions and risk-averse investment behaviors (e.g.,Malmendier and Nagel 2011;Knüpfer, Rantapuska, and Sarvimäki 2017). In the U.S. corporate setting,Dittmar and Duchin (2015)find that firms with CEOs who have worked at a company that has undergone financial difficulties hold more cash, andSchoar and Zuo (2017)find that CEOs who began their careers during recessions tend to follow more conservative corporate policies. This mechanism is also likely to be general globally, as firms across many countries beyond Korea have experienced financial crises of comparable magnitude.

We study how treated firms with memories from the Asian financial crisis differ in their responses to the reform and whether the valuation effects differ. We define an indicator variableCrisisMemoryequal to one if either (i) the firm’s CEO was an executive of a firm during the Asian financial crisis that was operating in an industry that depends heavily on external financing, or (ii) the firm was established before the crisis and operates in an industry that depends heavily on external financing.29We define industries as being external-financing dependent (High-EFD) based onRajan and Zingales (1998)by calculating the average of capital expenditures less operating cash flow within each industry; we define the high external-finance-dependent industries as those above the median. Our use of this measure in the context of the Asian financial crisis is based onAlmeida, Kim, and Kim (2015), who find that Korean firms in these industries experienced more severe liquidity shocks during the crisis.

We first test the hypothesis that firms with a crisis memory held more cash before the reform, which would be consistent withCrisisMemorycapturing variation in a behavioral bias to accumulate high (possibly excessive) cash buffers. The alternative hypothesis is that managers in all firms know about the effects of the 1997 crisis regardless of whether they lived through it, and thus that corporations’ cash levels are independent of their history. In panel A ofTable A10in theInternet Appendix, we regress cash holdings onCrisisMemory, controlling for firm size and profitability. Consistent with our hypothesis, these results show that firms with a crisis memory held more cash in the years before the reform.

We next test whetherCrisisMemoryis related to firms’ responses regarding changes to cash retention and alternative uses. Conceptually, even though firms with a crisis memory held more cash before the reform, it is not obvious whether these firms should react more or less strongly. If, on the one hand, the reform acts as a nudge for these firms to retain less cash, we expect a stronger response for the firms with a crisis memory. On the other hand, if behavioral biases are so entrenched that they induce “lexicographic” preferences, firms might be impervious to nudges to change their behavior, in which case we would expect a weaker response by firms with a crisis memory.

In panel A ofTable 7, we test whether firms with a crisis memory responded differently to the reform by employing a triple-differences framework:

The main coefficient of interest isβ5⁠, which measures the extent to which treated firms with a crisis memory responded differently to the reform than other treated firms.Table 7shows that treated firms with a crisis memory reduced their cash retention more after the reform (column 1 of panel A), and these firms also raised payouts, investments, and wages more (columns 2–4). The economic magnitudes in these responses are about double, although some of these effects are only statistically significant at the 10%level.

As a robustness test, we confirm that these results do not merely reflect industry-level differences related to external financing dependence but are related to a firm’s or CEO’s past experiences. To do so, in panel A ofTable A11in theInternet Appendix, we report results from a placebo test where we replace theCrisisMemoryvariable with a variable indicating whether a firm operates in an industry with high dependence on external financing, but where neither the firm nor the CEO was active in 1997. These placebo tests show no relationship between firm responses to the reform and their dependence on external financing, indicating that the results inTable 7are driven by the interaction of both being dependent on external finance and having memories from the earlier crisis. Panel B ofTable A11in theInternet Appendixsimilarly shows a placebo test involving “old” CEOs or firms (defined as operating in 1997) but where the firm does not rely heavily on external financing.

We next examine differences in valuation effects. Given that firms with a crisis memory responded more strongly to the reform, and because the average valuation response was positive (Section4.1), we might expect investors to react more favorably to the reform for these firms. This is especially true if these firms’ pre-reform cash holdings were more likely to be excessive. The alternative hypothesis is that these firms’ cash holdings did not reflect a personal bias but instead reflected an optimal policy in correctly assessing the probability of a future severe downturn, in which case the market reaction should be more negative for these firms, given their stronger responses away from the previously optimal cash retention.30

In panel B ofTable 7, we examine the differences in the reactions by market participants for the treated crisis-memory firms versus treated firms without such a memory. These tests are based on the regression:

The results show that the estimated coefficient onTreated×CrisisMemory(⁠β3⁠) is positive, indicating that the positive cumulative abnormal returns around the reform for the treated firms are concentrated among firms with a crisis memory. These larger value effects are consistent with the hypothesis that these firms’ marginal dollars retained as cash were less valuable than in other firms and with these firms making greater changes to their policies.31

6.2 Corporate governance and agency conflicts

We next examine the role that corporate governance played in influencing firms’ responses and investors’ reactions to the reform. Previous research suggests that poor governance and agency conflicts are related to low payouts, high investments, and excessive cash holdings (e.g.,La Porta et al. 2000;Jensen 1986;Bertrand and Mullainathan 2003;Gao, Harford, and Li 2013;Nikolov and Whited 2014;Isakov, Pérignon, and Weisskopf 2021). Poor corporate governance has been shown to regularly afflict Korean firms, taking even such extreme forms as expropriation by the controlling families (e.g.,Bae, Kang, and Kim 2002;Baek, Kang, and Lee 2006). Yet, this is not a problem that is unique to Korea, as similar corporate governance concerns are common in many countries around the world (Morck, Wolfenzon, and Yeung 2005).

To measure corporate governance quality, we employ a governance score created by the Korea Corporate Governance Service (KCGS). KCGS calculates these scores every year for larger firms listed on the KOSPI market, the main bourse, and this measure is therefore limited to those firms. The score is calculated as a sum that encompasses four distinct aspects of governance: (i) the protection of shareholder rights; (ii) the internal processes of the board; (iii) the quality of monitoring organizations; (iv) transparency of disclosures. We define an indicator variableHigh-Gthat is equal to one if the firm has an above-median governance score. Note that higher scores here denote better governance, which is the opposite of the GIM-index or E-index scores in the United States where higher scores reflect lower governance quality. Consistent with the previous literature on the link between governance and cash holdings,Table A10(panel B) in theInternet Appendixshows that poorly governed firms (the base group in the regression) tended to hold relatively more cash before the reform than firms with better governance (High-G).

How did poorly and well-governed firms differ in their responses to the reform? Conceptually, even though poorly governed firms tend to have more cash, we posit that using the tax code to incentivize these firms to retain less cash may not result in better outcomes. This is because the alternative uses of this money are likely to be less efficient for poorly governed firms; for example, these firms may be prone to empire-building.

In panel A ofTable 8, we report results from triple-differences regressions that describe how corporate governance is related to post-reform changes in cash retention, payouts, investments, and wage increases for the treated firms. These regressions follow the same framework as Equation (4).

Column 1 first shows no statistically significant differences between the poorly and well-governed firms regarding their changes to cash retention. However, consistent with an empire-building hypothesis, columns 2–4 show that these groups of firms differed dramatically in how they allocated the money toward alternative uses. Treated firms with poor governance did not increase payouts to shareholders, and the higher average payouts (see Section5) were thus concentrated among the well-governed treated firms. Instead, the poorly governed treated firms spent significantly more on investments and wage increases, while the treated firms that were well-governed did not increase spending on either investments or wages.32

Finally, we test whether investors reacted differently to the reform depending on a firm’s governance. Panel B ofTable 8shows that investors’ reaction to the reform was lower and statistically insignificant from zero for poorly governed treated firms. By contrast, the market reaction was higher and statistically significant only for the well-governed treated firms. This suggests that investors also expected the better-governed firms to respond more efficiently to the reform.

This evidence is broadly consistent with empire-building among poorly governed firms. Instead of retaining cash, poorly governed firms switched toward other forms of internal investment but did not pay out more to shareholders. An empire-building hypothesis can also explain why the market reaction was lower. These results reveal a potential dark side of the tax reform: even if some firms were retaining too much cash because of agency conflicts, pushing these firms to do something else with that money does not necessarily improve firm value if the money is not spent well.
article.7. Robustness and Additional Evidence:
7.1 Intensity of treatment

Treated firms with high earnings are affected more intensely by the reform as they need to make larger changes to their financial and investment policies to minimize or avoid the new tax. We thus expect these firms to respond more strongly and that their values are more affected if investors anticipate this differential response.

For example, in column 2, the baseline coefficient onTreated, which captures the abnormal returns for treated low-earnings firms, is not significantly different from zero. By contrast, treated firms with high earnings experience significantly higher returns. These results are consistent with investors anticipating that high-earnings firms would need to change their behavior to a greater extent to avoid or minimize the new tax, while low-earnings firms would not need to change their behavior much.

If anything, we tend to observe pre-trends that run opposite to our main results; for example, for the investment outcome in the 10–90 billion won sample, the treated firms exhibit slightly lower trends in investment in the years leading up to the reform.

7.3 Chaebol versus non-chaebol firms

We next exploit the “two-dimensionality” in the assignment of treatment—that is, that treatment is based on satisfying at least one of two inclusion criteria. This can help address a concern whereby the parallel trends assumption could be violated if chaebol firms follow different trends unrelated to the reform.

In panel A ofTable 11, we exclude all chaebol firms in the difference-in-differences tests. For the remaining non-chaebol firms, whether a firm is treated thus depends exclusively on whether its book equity is above or below 50 billion won, and we further limit the sample to those that fall in the 10–90 billion won range. In these tests, the results remain similar to our previous baseline findings, implying that any possible trend differences between chaebol and non-chaebol firms do not drive the previous results.

Further, even within the relatively narrow range of book equity between 10 billion and 90 billion won, we might still be concerned that the slightly larger firms might follow a different trend unrelated to the reform. We address this concern in several ways. In panel B ofTable 11, we conduct a placebo test by limiting the sample to only chaebol firms in the 10–90 billion won range, and we then conduct a difference-in-differences test around the 50 billion won threshold—this threshold should not matter among any of the firms within this sample, as the reform applied to all chaebol firms irrespective of size. Our findings show that the 50 billion threshold does not predict differential changes in the main outcome variables for chaebol firms. For investment, we even find an effect opposite to what we observe in our baseline results. These findings support the parallel trends assumption since if spurious confounders existed related to whether firms had book equity above or below 50 billion won, we might expect such confounders also to affect chaebol firms in a similar way.

As a robustness test for the valuation effects from Section4.1, we also repeat this analysis while excluding the chaebol firms (Table A13in theInternet Appendix). This helps address a possibility that there might have existed expectations before the announcement of the reform that chaebols would be affected by such a reform, and the positive announcement effect could have reflected some resolution of regulatory uncertainty for these firms. This analysis shows that the baseline results remain after dropping chaebols, suggesting that an anticipation effect for chaebol firms does not confound the baseline valuation effect.

7.4 Additional controls

An alternative way to account for possible confounding effects related to book equity is by employing a regression discontinuity difference-in-differences (RD-DD) framework. To this end, we include a control for the distance to the threshold (Book equity – 50 billion won), interacted withAfter, in the baseline regressions. We also include additional control variables,sales,sales growth,net income, andleverage, all interacted withAfter. These results, reported in panel C ofTable 11, show that the estimated effects remain similar or become even stronger when accounting for the distance to the threshold and other possible covariates (the coefficients on the control variables are omitted, but the full results are reported inTable A14in theInternet Appendix).

We next consider whether firms close to the threshold intentionally shrink their book equity to avoid the tax and how such behavior might affect our results. We address this concern in two ways. First, we examine how many firms move up or down across the 50 billion won threshold to examine whether an abnormally high number of firms shrink to just below, consistent with avoiding treatment. We find little evidence that firms are shrinking to avoid being subject to the tax: while 39 firms shrank from above to below the threshold between 2014 and 2015 (and thus avoided treatment), 185 firms grew from below to above (and thus became treated); the fact that more firms are growing is consistent with overall growth in the economy over this period. We thus observe no widespread trend that firms intentionally shrink to avoid treatment, presumably because the main way to reduce book equity to avoid treatment is to make payouts to shareholders, which is also one of the ways that firms can avoid paying the tax even if they are treated. Second, we reestimate our results using a “Donut RD” empirical strategy, where we focus on the 10–90 billion won range but exclude firms in the 45–55 billion won range for whom the incentives to shrink to avoid the tax may be the strongest. The results reported inTable A15in theInternet Appendixare similar and, if anything, generally stronger than in the baseline tests.

7.5 Cross-sectional regressions within narrower sample and matching sample

As a final robustness test, we replicate our cross-sectional analyses from Sections6and7.1within the narrower sample that only includes firms in the 10–90 billion range. The replication ofTables 7(Crisis Memory Channel) and 9 (Intensity of Treatment) using this narrower sample are reported inTables A16andA17in theInternet Appendix. These results are broadly similar to the full sample results. We nevertheless cannot similarly study the effects of a governance channel (as inTable 8) within only the narrower 10–90 billion won sample; the reason is that the governance score is only available for larger listed firms, which means that there are very few observations remaining within the narrower window with available governance data.

Similarly, we replicate the cross-sectional analyses using the matching sample where each treated firm is matched to a “nearest neighbor” untreated control firm. The replication ofTables 7,8, and9(for Crisis Memory Channel, Governance Channel, and Intensity of Treatment, respectively) using the matching sample are reported inTables A18,A19, andA20, respectively, in theInternet Appendix.33Note that unlike for the 10–90 billion sample, we retain a sufficient number of observations with available governance scores for the matched sample to enable this analysis. We find that the results are similar to the full sample results.
article.8. Conclusion:
Corporations worldwide have accumulated record levels of cash on their balance sheets. Many observers claim this trend has gone too far and criticize firms for “hoarding” cash. Could this money be put to more productive uses if firms retained less cash and spent more on payouts, wages, or new investments?

This paper investigates these questions by exploiting a tax reform in South Korea that sought to curb corporate cash accumulation by imposing a new tax on cash retention. Our findings contribute to the literature in two principal ways. First, we use the natural experiment enabled by this policy intervention to contribute to the literature on the value of corporate cash. Second, by shedding light on the consequences of a unique policy intervention, this setting can offer valuable lessons for future policies that seek to influence firms’ financial decisions.

Our findings show that, on average, firms subject to the reform decreased cash retention and spent more on payouts, wage increases, and investments. Whether retaining less cash is desirable depends on whether the treated firms’ cash and investment policies were optimal before the reform and whether the spending on alternative uses is efficient. We use an event study methodology to show that the valuation consequences of the reform were positive, implying that investors expected firms’ responses to be sufficiently value-improving to offset any direct negative valuation consequences from paying higher taxes. These results are consistent with firms exhibiting excessive cash retention before the reform.

When we examine two possible channels that could explain why some firms retain too much cash on the margin, we find evidence consistent with both behavioral biases and agency conflicts. Specifically, consistent with behavioral bias, firms that were more likely to have adverse memories from the Asian financial crisis used to hold more cash before the reform, but these firms also responded more strongly by making larger cuts to their cash retention, and investors reacted more positively. Consistent with agency conflicts, firms with weaker governance tended to hold more cash before the reform, but these poorly governed firms did not increase payouts in response to the reform. Instead, poorly governed firms allocate more toward investments and experience relatively lower valuations, consistent with empire-building. An important takeaway is that using policy levers to discourage corporate cash retention is not a silver bullet, as the consequences will depend on why firms were retaining so much cash in the first place and how they allocate this money to alternative uses.

Appendix A

Calendar-time portfolio analysis

This appendix discusses an alternative methodology and results based on analyzing valuation effects using a long-short calendar-time portfolio approach.

The reason for considering this methodology is that, despite the inclusion of industry fixed effects in the event study regressions, there could remain some cross-correlations in the event returns that could influence the statistical inference, especially because the event dates are the same across all firms. As a robustness test, we therefore employ a portfolio method where we form portfolios of the treated and untreated firms and examine the returns of these portfolios around the event dates (Eckbo, Nygaard, and Thorburn 2022). We calculate daily returns for a long-minus-short portfolio that is long treated firms and short untreated firms, and then run the regression:
